seasonality: Rainy or very hot days should lean toward museums and shorter outdoor landmark segments.
Mild weather supports adding Central Park or a short Midtown walk between indoor stops.
transport_advice: Group Manhattan museums and landmarks by neighborhood to avoid unnecessary borough transfers.
Use subway lines or short rideshare hops between Upper Manhattan museum clusters and Midtown landmarks.
planning_tips: Choose one or two major museums rather than stacking every famous museum into the same day.
Pair Central Park with nearby museum stops when the traveler wants culture plus outdoor breaks.
Keep Midtown landmarks as a compact add-on instead of mixing them with distant Brooklyn food routes.
title: New York Museums, Landmarks, and Culture Planning Notes
content: Seed benchmark knowledge for New York culture routes: Manhattan museum clusters, classic landmarks, and Central Park work best when grouped by neighborhood. A strong first-time route should balance major indoor museums with nearby outdoor or landmark stops instead of scattering the day across all five boroughs.

seasonality: Cold or rainy days should keep food stops near subway access and reduce long outdoor walks.
Warm evenings support outdoor neighborhood walks and waterfront food pairings.
transport_advice: Keep food neighborhoods clustered instead of sending travelers between Manhattan, Brooklyn, and Queens in the same short block.
Use direct subway lines to start and end the route near the hotel or a major transfer hub.
planning_tips: For food requests, pick one or two neighborhoods and preserve time for browsing, snacks, and unscheduled stops.
Pair Chinatown or the Lower East Side with nearby downtown walking rather than uptown museums.
For Brooklyn food routes, avoid repeated borough hopping and keep the evening return simple.
title: New York Food, Neighborhoods, and Citywalk Planning Notes
content: Seed benchmark knowledge for New York food routes: food and neighborhood itineraries should favor depth in compact areas such as Chinatown, the Lower East Side, Greenwich Village, Williamsburg, or Chelsea Market. The planner should avoid turning a food walk into a scattered citywide transfer exercise.

seasonality: Windy or rainy days should shorten waterfront walks and add indoor backups nearby.
Clear evenings work well for skyline viewpoints or ferry-based harbor views.
transport_advice: Group parks and waterfront viewpoints by side of the city to avoid crossing the river repeatedly.
End scenic evenings near a direct subway, ferry, or rideshare route back to the hotel.
planning_tips: Use one primary skyline or waterfront viewpoint instead of stacking several distant view stops.
Pair the High Line with Chelsea or Hudson River stops, and pair Brooklyn Bridge Park with nearby food or waterfront time.
Keep Central Park as a pacing buffer when the traveler wants outdoor time between cultural stops.
title: New York Parks, Waterfront, and Skyline View Planning Notes
content: Seed benchmark knowledge for New York scenic routes: parks, waterfront walks, ferry views, and skyline stops should be treated as weather-sensitive anchors. A coherent scenic route should choose a compact park or waterfront cluster and leave time for transit back to the hotel.
